title: First and Last Position of Target
description:
Given a sorted array of integers, find the starting and ending position of a given target value. If the target is not found, return [-1, -1]. Use two tailored binary searches to find the leftmost and rightmost occurrences.

    

Constraints:
    - 0 ≤ len(nums) ≤ 10^5
- -10^9 ≤ nums[i], target ≤ 10^9
explanation:
Intuition
A single binary search finds any occurrence, but we need the extremes. A left-biased search returns the first index where target could appear. A right-biased search returns the first index greater than target. Combine them to produce the final range.

Approach
1. Initial Setup: Write a helper that returns the first index where value is ≥ target.
2. Main Algorithm: Run it with left bias on target and again with left bias on target+ side to get the right boundary.
3. Return Result: Validate the left boundary actually hits the target then report [left, right-1].

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(log n) because the search space halves at each step.
- Space Complexity: O(1) since only a few variables are maintained.

Key Insights
- Separate searches avoid linear scans around matches
- Binary search can be tuned with bias conditions

Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting to check existence before using indices
- Off by one errors on the right boundary
